perturbation.original_text: Unless terminated in accordance with the provisions of Section 10.2 below, the term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue in effect for a FIVE (5) year period.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless terminated in accordance with the provisions of Section 10.2 below, the term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue in effect for a TWENTY (20) year period.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the contract term to 20 years might raise concerns under California law regarding contracts that restrain trade, particularly if the agreement limits INTERSECT's ability to source API from other suppliers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While a supply agreement doesn't inherently restrain trade, a very long term, combined with other restrictive terms (e.g., exclusive supply), could be construed as doing so. A 20-year exclusive supply agreement would severely restrict INTERSECT's options.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the contract term from 5 to 20 years. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this could violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally invalidates contracts that restrain trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While a supply agreement isn't inherently a restraint of trade, a 20-year term, especially if exclusive, could be construed as such. Therefore, the modified text creates a potential legal contradiction by increasing the risk of violating Section 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon the occurrence of any of the following events: (i) the failure of the other party to comply with any of the terms of this Agreement or otherwise discharge its duties hereunder in any material respect, or the breach by the other party of any of its representations or warranties herein in any material respect, if such failure or breach is not cured within ninety (90) days of such breaching party's receipt of written notice specifying the nature of such failure or breach with particularity; or (ii) the making by the other party of an assignment for the benefit of its creditors, or the filing by or against such other party of any petition under any federal, state or local bankruptcy, insolvency or similar laws, if such filing has not been stayed or dismissed within sixty (60) days after the date thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon the occurrence of any of the following events: (i) the failure of the other party to comply with any of the terms of this Agreement or otherwise discharge its duties hereunder in any material respect, or the breach by the other party of any of its representations or warranties herein in any material respect, if such failure or breach is not cured within thirty (30) days of such breaching party's receipt of written notice specifying the nature of such failure or breach with particularity; or (ii) the making by the other party of an assignment for the benefit of its creditors, or the filing by or against such other party of any petition under any federal, state or local bankruptcy, insolvency or similar laws, if such filing has not been stayed or dismissed within thirty (30) days after the date thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 90 to 30 days for a material breach may be considered unconscionable or against public policy in certain circumstances under California law, especially if the breach involves complex issues requiring more time to resolve.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly specifying a time period, California Commercial Code Section 1201 defines 'good faith' as honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. A 30-day cure period may be deemed commercially unreasonable in certain situations. Specifically, if a complex breach would require more than 30 days to fix, this could be considered an unfair dealing. Depending on the breach, the 90 days specified is much more reasonable.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential legal contradiction. The original contract provided a 90-day cure period for breaches, whereas the modified contract reduces this to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests that a 30-day period may be deemed commercially unreasonable in certain situations, particularly if the breach involves complex issues needing more time to resolve. California Commercial Code Section 1201, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines 'good faith' as including the observance of 'reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.' A cure period that is too short to reasonably address a complex breach could be argued as a failure to act in good faith, creating a legal contradiction in terms of commercially reasonable standards. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (cure period) does not align with legal requirements (good faith and fair dealing), potentially making the contractual term unenforceable.
